corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: In this paper, we describe a scalable end- to-end tree boosting system called XGBoost, which is used widely by data scientists to achieve state-of-the-art results on many machine learning challenges.

top_candidates.sentence: In order to do so, a split finding algorithm enumerates over all the possible splits on all the features.
Most existing single machine tree boosting implementations, such as scikit-learn , R's gbm as well as the single machine version of XGBoost support the exact greedy algorithm.

In order to do so efficiently, the algorithm must first sort the data according to feature values and visit the data in sorted order to accumulate the gradient statistics for the structure score in Eq .
The exact greedy algorithm is very powerful since it enumerates over all possible splitting points greedily.
To support effective gradient tree boosting in these two settings, an approximate algorithm is needed.
We summarize an approximate framework, which resembles the ideas proposed in past literatures , in Alg.
To summarize, the algorithm first proposes candidate splitting points according to percentiles of feature distribution (a specific criteria will be given in Sec.
The algorithm then maps the continuous features into buckets split by these candidate points, aggregates the statistics and finds the best solution among proposals based on the aggregated statistics.
There are two variants of the algorithm, depending on when the proposal is given.
Most existing approximate algorithms for distributed tree learning also follow this framework.
Our system efficiently supports exact greedy for the single machine setting, as well as approximate algorithm with both local and global proposal methods for all settings.
The first dataset we use is the Allstate insurance claim dataset 8 .
This dataset is used to evaluate the impact of sparsity-aware algorithm in Sec.
The second dataset is the Higgs boson dataset 9 from high energy physics.
It contains 21 kinematic properties measured by the particle detectors in the accelerator.
The third dataset is the Yahoo! learning to rank challenge dataset , which is one of the most commonly used benchmarks in learning to rank algorithms.
The dataset contains 20K web search queries, with each query corresponding to a list of around 22 documents.
The last dataset is the criteo terabyte click log dataset 10 .
We use this dataset to evaluate the scaling property of the system in the out-of-core and the distributed settings.
We use the first three datasets for the single machine parallel setting, and the last dataset for the distributed and out-of-core settings. able cores in the machine.
For this type of experiment, it is important to use a very large dataset to drain the system file cache for a real outof-core setting.
The dataset is stored on AWS S3 instead of HDFS to avoid purchasing persistent storage.
XGBoost can process the entire dataset using as little as four machines, and scales smoothly by utilizing more available resources.
We call this the exact greedy algorithm.
The exact greedy algorithm is shown in Alg.
